---
title: "How to serial interval?"
subtitle: "The onset-to-onset delay that links a transmission pair"
author: "Sam Abbott"
institute: "London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine"
title-slide-attributes:
  data-notes: ""
date: 2026-06-16
date-format: "D MMMM YYYY"
---

## Talk plan

- What is a serial interval, and why do we want it?
- A serial interval is a delay: censoring, truncation, and reporting
- Extending primary event censoring: more distributions, point sources
- A within-host informed approach, and generic alternatives
- Delay estimation in practice, and time-to-event thinking more broadly
- Composing the distributions
- How can you help me?

::: {.callout-note}
Lorentz Center workshop, *Connecting Survival Analysis and Infectious Disease
Modeling*, Leiden, 16 June 2026.
:::

{{< include _partials/01-intro.qmd >}}

{{< include _partials/02-censoring.qmd >}}

{{< include _partials/06-pointsource.qmd >}}

{{< include _partials/03-paper.qmd >}}

{{< include _partials/04-practice.qmd >}}

{{< include _partials/08-tte-broadly.qmd >}}

## What we can get from the data

- In my view the serial interval **should** rarely be the goal in
  itself
- It is a window onto transmission timing, and what we can estimate is
  set by how the data were collected
- Exposure windows, symptom-onset recall, contact-tracing structure,
  and real-time truncation all shape the answer
- Design the estimand around the data, not the data around a
  textbook serial interval

{{< include _partials/05-composed.qmd >}}

{{< include _partials/07-collaboration.qmd >}}

# Wrapping up

## Summary

- A serial interval is a delay observed through censoring and
  truncation: borrow the survival-analysis machinery
- Within-host informed models link it to the generation time and the
  biology; generic contact-interval models need less
- The same time-to-event thinking runs from delays to real-time
  outbreak size
- Applied work shows the estimate is only as good as the data
  collection behind it
- Composable distribution tooling lets us express the many ways a
  serial interval is built, and reuse them across models

## Thank you

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
- The serial interval is the onset-to-onset delay within a
  transmission pair, seen through censoring and truncation
- The interesting work is at the interface with survival analysis, and
  I would like to spend the afternoon there
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
- Slides: [samabbott.co.uk/how-to-serial-interval](https://samabbott.co.uk/how-to-serial-interval)
- [primarycensored](https://primarycensored.epinowcast.org/) ·
  [CensoredDistributions.jl](https://github.com/EpiAware/CensoredDistributions.jl)
:::
::::
